BO6 is currently not linux friendly, and I doubt activision cares enough about linux to support it in the future. Is linux 100% gaming ready? I would say yes, but the issue is that triple-a game companies and anticheat devs just dont care enough to support linux, and some games (rainbow 6 for example) will either refuse to boot if the game detects you're on linux or (gtav online for example) will just ban your account if it sees you playing on linux. Linux itself is perfectly fine and capable especially with proton to run windows applications. 

That being said, its a very small number of games that dont work, and if you really want an alternative solution to dual booting you can always create a windows vm and passthrough your gpu and it'll work just fine. 

Overall though, should you switch to linux? I say fuck it, go for it. I went balls deep and made the switch from windows to fedora in 2021, mainly because my pc was "too old" to suppirt windows 11, despite it being a 24 core machine with 128 gigs of ram lol. Proton back then was far more finnicky, and I had to tweak the launch settings for most games to get them to run (and some just didnt at all). Nowadays however, there isnt a single game in my steam library that doesnt just work perfectly fine out of the box on proton 9. Linux gaming has come a long way in the last few years, and its only gonna get better.

One matter that is never discussed, but that often kicks companies in the balls, is the network cost of microservices.

By nature, microservices communicate with each other. And the more each service is used, the more it "talks" to others. And the more load you have, the more they "talk" to each other.

Exponentially.

And while networking is dirt cheap nowadays, exponential curves are what they are. And your much awaited "scale up moment" moment may very well cost you tens of thousands in networking. And a slow as fuck application, because your microservices flood each other.

This is slightly less true nowadays, as everyone hosts on cloud platforms. But it's still true that companies never see the flooding and the bills coming...

She’s 13. You’re the adult. Some things have to be led by you. This is one of those things.
text:
Her daughter is scared. Yes, action needs to be taken, but there's nothing wrong with tempering that reaction and including her daughter in the dialogue and working to get her on board. Going immediately nuclear while her daughter is freaking out about how this will affect her - without taking the time to give her a chance to process it and get on board - could break her trust in her parents. They might "solve" this problem only to ensure their daughter never trusts them with problems like this again.

You don't do nothing, but you also don't take a wrecking ball to her trust and wreck the relationship along with the problem. The statute of limitations on what he did is definitely long enough for OP to gain that trust before taking an action that is going to have a profound effect on their daughter's life.

Let's be real about what's going to happen here: she's going to have to talk to police, possibly testify in court, be asked really hard and uncomfortable questions by a defense attorney, she's going to be viewed by a non-zero number of adults in her life as a troublemaker, she's going to become a social pariah at her school, she'll deal with bullying and name-calling and rumors being spread about her, she'll deal with people who will be inexplicably on ***HIS*** side getting in her face and being shitty and bullying her - in short, this is going to ruin school life for her, outside of her true closest friends and those adults who actually give enough of a fuck to care. And we've *already* seen that there's more than a couple of adults in this situation who would rather make it go away than be of any actual help.

Give them a little bit of grace for not being so quick to dismiss how their daughter feels about what's coming. She deserves to have ***at least*** her parents being considerate of her feelings, since nobody else is.
